ac:rich-text-body.p: Flowthrough Archive data is a subset of calibrated visibility data archived continuously for re-processing and serendipitous science discovery.

p: Flowthrough Archive (FTA) data is an Observatory Data Product consisting of a reduced-resolution subset of calibrated interferometric visibilities archived continuously during SKA observations. Unlike full visibility data, which may be prohibitively large to store permanently, FTA data provides a balanced approach: permanent archival of scientifically valuable raw data while managing storage constraints.
The Flowthrough Archive serves multiple scientific goals:

ul.li: Re-processing Capability: Enable researchers to re-process observations with improved algorithms or different parameters without requiring the original observing time
Serendipitous Discovery: Enable detection of unexpected scientific phenomena in data collected for different primary purposes
Long-term Preservation: Ensure that raw data supporting published science remain available for independent verification and reanalysis
Archive Completeness: Build a comprehensive record of SKA observations for the astronomical community
Visibility Data: Complex-valued Fourier-space measurements (real and imaginary components)
Calibration: Applied gain, phase, and flux density calibration
Metadata: Observation parameters, antenna positions, system temperatures, RFI flags

p: To make permanent archival feasible, FTA data uses reduced spectral and temporal resolution compared to full visibility data:

ul.li: Frequency Resolution: Typically 10–100 times coarser than native pipeline resolution (channels combined via averaging)
Time Resolution: Typically 1–10 second integrations instead of sub-second samples
Baseline Information: All baselines preserved, enabling independent imaging and analysis

p: These reductions yield approximately 10–100× data volume savings while retaining sufficient information for most re-processing and serendipitous science use cases.
FTA data preserves full polarisation information (XX, XY, YX, YY or equivalent) enabling Stokes parameter reconstruction and polarimetric analysis.
Unlike higher-level data products (images, catalogues) that may be selective, FTA data are archived continuously for all observations, ensuring:

ul.li: No observation is excluded due to perceived quality or science priority
Unexpected or serendipitous discoveries are not hindered by selective archival policies
The scientific legacy of SKA observations is fully preserved
Retention: All FTA data are retained indefinitely in the SKA Archive
Tiering: Data may be moved from fast access (disk) to slower cold storage (tape) after a specified period, but remain accessible
Redundancy: Multiple copies are maintained at geographically distributed archive sites

h2: How FTA Differs from Full Visibility Data
p: The SKA produces two visibility-based products:

tr.td: May not be permanently archived for all observations
Very large volume (100 TB to multi-PB per observation hour)
Smaller volume (1–10 TB per observation hour)
Optimal for targeted re-processing and sensitivity-limited science
Suitable for general re-processing and serendipitous analysis

ul.li: Apply custom calibration and imaging algorithms to archived observations
Investigate systematic effects or improve known sources with new techniques
Test hypothetical observing strategies or data reduction approaches

p: Examples of serendipitous discoveries enabled by FTA archival:

ul.li: Detection of unexpected transients in survey data collected for other purposes
Identification of new bright sources or unusual objects in archival datasets
Population studies enabled by mining large archival samples

p: FTA data enable researchers to correlate transient detections or variability across multiple observation epochs, even when original observations targeted different fields or science goals.
The SKA Archive provides search and filtering for FTA data by:

ul.li: Date and time of observation
Sky coordinates (RA/Dec) and field size
Frequency coverage and resolution
Integration time
Observation metadata (observer, project, science category)
Direct download: Download FTA datasets via the Archive portal or command-line tools
Streaming: Access subsets of large files without full download (e.g., specific frequency range or time window)
Cloud access: Analyse FTA data in situ using SKA computational services

p: FTA data are provided in standard radio astronomy formats (MS, FITS, HDF5) compatible with common analysis software including CASA, AIPS, and custom pipelines.

ol.li: Discover relevant FTA observation(s) via Archive search
Download or stream FTA visibility data
Apply calibration and imaging algorithms using CASA, AIPS, or custom scripts
Validate results against published ODPs or alternative processing
Publish results or advanced data products derived from FTA re-processing

p: FTA data volumes are manageable for re-processing on modest computational resources:

ul.li: Single-machine processing possible for typical observations (disk-based or cloud)
Large surveys may benefit from distributed computing via cloud services
Example Jupyter notebooks and container images provided to accelerate analysis

ac:rich-text-body.p: FTA data's reduced resolution may not be suitable for all science cases. Researchers requiring full spectral or temporal resolution should consult the Archive documentation on full visibility data availability.
See Introduction to Observatory Data Products and Advanced Data Products for an overview of the complete ODP suite.
